--- Presentation - Transforming BiteDash into Microservices.pdf ---
IT22579768
Transforming 
BiteDash into 
Microservices

Introduction to 
BiteDash
BiteDash is an innovative online food delivery 
platform serving millions of users. Its rapid growth 
has led to significant technical challenges that 
necessitate a transition to a more robust 
architecture.

Current System: 
Monolithic 
Architecture
The existing architecture is a single codebase
managing all modules, relying on a shared 
database and tightly coupled components that 
hinder scalability and flexibility, limiting BiteDash's 
ability to adapt to growth.

4
Current System Challenges
Addressing performance and scalability issues
Performance Issues
During peak hours, the monolithic 
architecture suffers from slow 
performance, leading to frustrating 
user experiences and potential loss of 
customers due to delayed service.
Scaling Difficulties
Scaling the entire system is 
expensive and inefficient, as it 
requires significant resources and 
infrastructure upgrades rather than 
allowing for targeted and cost-
effective scaling of services.
Deployment Risks
Deployments in a monolithic setup 
are difficult and risky, as changes 
to one component can impact the 
entire system, leading to potential 
downtimes and service disruptions.

5
Challenges in Monolith
System issues hindering progress
System Failures
System-wide failures can disrupt all 
operations, leading to significant 
downtime and affecting user 
experience. The interconnected nature 
of the monolith exacerbates these 
issues.
Feature Limitations
Adding new features becomes a 
complex processthat risks 
introducing bugs or breaking existing 
functionalities, making innovation 
slow and cumbersome in a tightly 
coupled architecture.
Deployment Risks
Deployments are inherently risky; 
changes made to one part of the 
system can inadvertently impact 
other components, leading to 
unexpected failures and user 
dissatisfaction.

6
Benefits of Microservices
Enhancing BiteDash's Architecture and Operations
Independent Services
Microservices operate as independent 
units, allowing teams to deploy, 
manage, and scale them without 
affecting other services, facilitating 
faster updates and enhancing overall 
system reliability.
Safer Deployments
With microservices, each service can 
be deployed individually. This 
reduces the risk associated with 
deployments, minimizing downtime 
and allowing for continuous 
integration and delivery practices.
Improved Scalability
Microservices architecture allows 
BiteDash to achieve better 
scalabilityby enabling individual 
services to scale independently 
based on their specific loads and 
demands, optimizing resource usage 
effectively.

7
Advantages of Microservices
Enhancing flexibility and reliability
Fault Isolation
Microservices architecture enables 
each service to operate independently, 
ensuring that failures in one service do 
not impact others, significantly 
improving overall system reliability and 
user experience.
Technology Flexibility
Teams can choose the best 
technology stack for each 
microservice, allowing for innovation 
and adaptation to new technologies 
without disrupting the entire system, 
enhancing long-term sustainability.
Independent Scaling
Each microservice can scale 
independently based on demand, 
optimizing resource allocation and 
cost while maintaining performance, 
making it easier to accommodate 
growth and fluctuating user activity.

•API Gateway: Single entry point for all client 
requests
•Auth Service: Manages user authentication 
and authorization
•Customer Service: Handles customer profiles 
and data management
Proposed 
Microservices 
Overview

•Restaurant Service: Manages restaurant data 
and menus efficiently
•Order Service: Handles order processing and 
status updates
•Payment Service: Facilitates secure payment 
transactions for users
Proposed 
Microservices 
Overview

•Delivery Service: Manages logistics and 
tracking for deliveries
•Review Service: Handles user-generated 
content and feedback
•Notification Service: Sends alerts and updates 
to users
Proposed 
Microservices 
Overview

Communication 
Patterns
Effective communication between microservices is essential 
for system efficiency. REST APIs enable synchronous 
interactions while a message broker facilitates asynchronous 
messaging, allowing for an event-driven architecturethat 
enhances decoupling and scalability.

Event-Driven 
Workflow
In this workflow, the customer places an order, triggering 
the Payment Service to process payment. Once confirmed, 
the Delivery Service schedules the delivery, ensuring a 
seamless user experience throughout the process.

Database Ownership 
Principle
Each microservice is designed to own its own database, 
ensuring independence and scalability. This architecture 
avoids shared databases, enhancing fault isolation and 
improving overall system reliability and performance.

Migration 
Strategy 
Overview
The Strangler Patternallows BiteDash to 
gradually transition from a monolithic architecture 
to microservices, enabling both systems to coexist, 
minimizing risks, and ensuring minimal downtime 
during the migration process.

Infrastructure Setup
The first step involves establishing 
the necessary infrastructure and 
monitoring tools to support the 
microservices architecture, ensuring 
that all components are properly 
configured and operational.
Extract Low-Risk Services
High-Traffic Services
Initially, low-risk services are 
extracted from the monolithic 
architecture, allowing for testing and 
refinement within the microservices 
framework without significant impact 
on overall operations.
Finally, high-traffic and complex 
services are migrated, optimizing the 
system's performance by leveraging 
the improved scalability and reliability 
of microservices, ensuring seamless 
user experiences.
Core Business Services
Following the initial extractions, core 
business services are transitioned, 
ensuring that critical functionalities 
are maintained and enhanced within 
the new microservices architecture 
for better performance.
Migration Phases

Final Code Freeze
The development team executed a 
final code freezefor the monolithic 
application, ensuring no new features 
would be added, focusing solely on 
transitioning to microservices.
Migration Completion
Monolith Shutdown
All essential services were 
successfully migrated to 
microservices, enabling independent 
deployment and scaling, which 
significantly improved both 
performance and reliability across 
the BiteDash platform.
The final shutdown of the monolithic 
application took place, marking a 
significant milestone in BiteDash's 
journey towards a more agile and 
efficient microservices architecture.
System Validation
Comprehensive testing and 
validation of the new microservices 
architecture were conducted to 
ensure functionality, performance, 
and system integrity before fully 
decommissioning the legacy 
monolith.
Decommissioning the Monolith

Achieving Future 
Growth
Embracing microservices architecture positions BiteDash
for future growth, ensuring scalable, resilient, and flexible 
systems that foster innovation while enabling faster feature 
delivery, ultimately enhancing user experience and 
engagement.
